data.title: Hacksaw (OM:HACK) Earnings Power Story Centers On 67.7% Net Margin Narrative Test
data.description:
Hacksaw (OM:HACK) just posted its Q1 2026 scorecard, with investors zeroing in on revenue and EPS trends after a year in which trailing twelve month revenue reached €197.5 million and basic EPS came in at €0.50. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from €37.3 million in Q3 2024 to €55.1 million in Q4 2025. Over that stretch, basic EPS ranged from €0.11 to €0.15 as net income excluding extra items moved between €25.7 million and €42.0 million, setting up Q1 against a backdrop of consistently high margins and earnings power.

See our full analysis for Hacksaw.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings run rate lines up with the most common stories investors tell about Hacksaw and where those narratives might need updating.

See what the community is saying about Hacksaw

Advertisement

High margins steady at about 68%

  • Over the last 12 months, net profit margin sat at 67.7%, slightly above the prior year level of 67.3%, which lines up with the high net income figures in the trailing revenue base of €197.5 million.
  • Supporters of the bullish view point to this margin profile as a key strength, arguing that very high gross margin on in house games and 89% free cash flow conversion can keep earnings power resilient even as the OpenRGS model grows.
    • What stands out is that bullish assumptions call for margins to rise from 64.1% to 76.0% over three years, while the current trailing net margin already sits at 67.7%. The latest numbers partly support those expectations but also raise the bar for them.
    • With trailing earnings up 44.8% year over year, the bullish case leans heavily on this combination of high profitability and strong cash generation to justify higher long term earnings targets.
Bulls argue that Hacksaw's 67.7% net margin and strong cash conversion are the real story behind this earnings print, not just the top line, and they see that as the engine for future compounding. 🐂 Hacksaw Bull Case

P/E sits above industry but below peers

  • Hacksaw is trading on a 16.8x trailing P/E versus about 14.8x for the broader European Entertainment industry, while the cited peer average P/E is 80x. The company therefore sits between a modest premium to the industry and a steep discount to that peer group figure.
  • Skeptics in the bearish narrative focus on the risk that a richer P/E than the industry average and rising costs from headcount and third party content could limit how much investors are willing to pay for the stock.
    • The current 16.8x P/E is only slightly above the 14.8x industry number, which challenges the idea of an overly stretched multiple. Bears, however, tie their caution to the prospect that OpenRGS carries lower margins and could gradually weigh on EBIT margin if it grows faster than in house games.
    • At the same time, the stock trades at €84.3 against an analyst price target of SEK103.44 and a DCF fair value of SEK304.73, so the valuation debate is really about whether high margins and earnings growth are enough to justify any premium to the industry while still looking inexpensive versus those reference points.
Skeptics point out that a 16.8x P/E above the industry average leaves less room for error if margins soften, even with reference values suggesting more upside. 🐻 Hacksaw Bear Case

Fast trailing earnings growth underpins forecasts

  • Over the last year, earnings grew 44.8% while analysts are using roughly 21.5% annual earnings growth and 24.1% annual revenue growth in their forecasts, which means the trailing pace has been much faster than the growth path currently built into expectations.
  • The consensus narrative leans on Hacksaw’s expanding game portfolio from 184 to 268 titles, rising OpenRGS contribution and 89% free cash flow conversion as reasons why above market revenue growth and high margins could support those forecast numbers.
    • What is interesting is that analysts expect margins to rise from 64.1% to 77.3% over three years while the net margin figure is already 67.7%, so the current level supports the consensus story but also means future gains need to come from an already high base.
    • With the stock at €84.3 and the analyst price target at SEK103.44, the gap between trailing earnings strength and more moderate forward growth assumptions is a key area for you to weigh when thinking about how durable this pace might be.

data.title: Volkswagen Court Challenge Tests Scale Of UK Car Finance Liabilities
data.description:
• Volkswagen has filed a legal challenge against the UK Financial Conduct Authority's proposed £9b car finance redress scheme.
  • The company is seeking independent clarification on how compensation rules should apply to its historical car finance agreements.
  • The case has potential financial implications for Volkswagen and may influence how auto finance complaints are handled across the sector.

For shareholders, this legal move comes at a time when Volkswagen, XTRA:VOW3, is already under pressure in the market. The share price stands at €85.48, with returns showing a 19.7% decline year to date and a 39.1% decline over five years. This backdrop makes any large, uncertain liability especially important to watch.

Looking ahead, the court process around the FCA scheme is likely to be lengthy and closely followed by the market. Investors may pay particular attention to any guidance on potential cost ranges, timing of cash outflows, and whether other manufacturers face similar exposure, as these factors could influence sentiment toward XTRA:VOW3 and the wider auto finance space.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Volkswagen by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Volkswagen.

Is Volkswagen's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

This legal challenge puts a sharper question mark over Volkswagen’s potential exposure to the UK car finance redress scheme. Instead of accepting the £9b framework as set out by the regulator, the company is asking the courts to define how compensation rules should apply to its own historical agreements. For you as an investor, the key issue is not only the final bill but also how predictable that liability becomes. A clearer ruling could narrow the range of outcomes for future cash outflows, while an adverse interpretation could point to higher provisions and pressure on returns if costs fall heavily on lenders and manufacturers.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Volkswagen Narrative

  • The focus in the narrative on regulatory and geopolitical headwinds is reinforced by this UK case, as it underlines how non-operational issues can affect cash generation and capital allocation.
  • The push for tariff relief and operational restructuring could be harder to translate into higher flexibility if Volkswagen has to ring fence cash for redress, especially when peers such as Mercedes-Benz, BMW and Stellantis are also juggling regulatory costs.
  • The narrative around trade disputes and tariffs does not directly address large-scale consumer finance remediation, so the potential size and timing of UK compensation payments may not be fully reflected in that story.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Volkswagen to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 4 key risks for Volkswagen, and this court case adds another regulatory overhang that could affect debt metrics if cash flow is diverted to customer remediation.
  • ⚠️ A broad ruling against car finance providers might encourage further complaints or closer scrutiny in other markets, which could weigh on auto finance economics for manufacturers such as Volkswagen, Mercedes-Benz and Stellantis.
  • 🎁 By seeking independent clarification now, Volkswagen may secure a more predictable framework for handling complaints, which can help long term planning for its financial services arm.
  • 🎁 If the court limits the scope of the FCA scheme, the eventual redress bill for Volkswagen and the wider sector could be lower than the headline £9b figure suggests, freeing up capital for other uses.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on the legal timetable, any interim court findings on how historical commissions and interest rates should be assessed, and whether Volkswagen discloses updated provisioning ranges in future earnings calls. It is also worth tracking how other manufacturers such as Mercedes-Benz respond, as a sector wide settlement or a common legal precedent could emerge. Any guidance from Volkswagen on balance sheet capacity, refinancing plans or potential rating implications will help you judge how this regulatory issue sits alongside other pressures like tariffs, electric-vehicle investment and brand specific performance.

data.description:

Approach 1: Compagnie Générale des Établissements Michelin Société en commandite par actions Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using an appropriate rate. It is essentially asking what the future stream of cash is worth in today’s money.

For Compagnie Générale des Établissements Michelin Société en commandite par actions, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about €1.78b. Analyst projections and Simply Wall St extrapolations suggest free cash flow between 2026 and 2035 stays in the billion euro range, reaching an estimated €3.17b in 2030 before further extrapolated figures.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €70.61 per share. Compared with the recent share price of €31.15, this implies the shares trade at a 55.9% discount to the DCF estimate, which indicates that the stock appears significantly undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Compagnie Générale des Établissements Michelin Société en commandite par actions is undervalued by 55.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 236 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Compagnie Générale des Établissements Michelin Société en commandite par actions.

Approach 2: Compagnie Générale des Établissements Michelin Société en commandite par actions Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings that support it. It captures how the market prices the business relative to its profits, which is often a core anchor for long term investors.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/E depends on expectations for earnings growth and the risks around those earnings. Higher growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one.

Compagnie Générale des Établissements Michelin Société en commandite par actions currently trades on a P/E of 12.82x. This sits below the Auto Components industry average P/E of 19.59x and close to the peer group average of 12.30x. Margins Set Back by One Off Loss

  • Over the last 12 months, net profit margin was 31.4% compared to 47.2% a year earlier, and that period included a one off loss of S$128.5 million that weighed on the reported profitability.
  • Bulls argue that income can grow over time as data center exposure deepens. However, the combination of a S$62.4 million net loss in FY 2026 Q4 and the one off S$128.5 million charge shows how quickly margins can be hit, which sits in tension with the idea of steadily improving economics.
    • The trailing 12 month net income of S$211.2 million is well below the S$338.9 million figure used by optimistic analysts, so the bullish view assumes a meaningful step up from what has just been reported.
    • Consensus narrative talks about profit margins moving from roughly 47.9% toward the mid 50% range over time. The latest year actually finished at 31.4%, so investors need to bridge a sizeable gap between current and hoped for margin levels.
Over the last year, that margin reset and the one off charge have become a key reference point for anyone weighing the more optimistic data center growth story against the current financial base. 🐂 Mapletree Industrial Trust Bull Case

Debt Coverage Stands Out As Key Risk

  • Debt coverage by operating cash flow is flagged as weak, and net profit margin for the last year at 31.4% is lower than the prior 47.2%, so the cushion to service borrowings has thinned at the same time as earnings were hit by the S$128.5 million one off loss.
  • Bears focus on this combination of softer profitability and leverage, arguing that higher borrowing costs and refinancing needs can pressure income even if properties remain largely occupied.
    • With leverage around 40% in the consensus description and cash flow coverage already described as a major risk, any further drag from interest expenses would eat directly into a trailing earnings base of S$211.2 million.
    • Concerns about segments such as business parks and certain data centers tie back to this, because slower rental growth or weaker reversions would make it harder to offset that financing burden with higher net property income.
Skeptics are therefore watching the combination of leverage and weaker margins closely, since it can limit how much room the trust has to absorb shocks or fund new projects without putting distributions under pressure. 🐻 Mapletree Industrial Trust Bear Case

Valuation Caught Between Yield And Growth Hopes

  • At a current unit price of S$1.97, the trust sits below the DCF fair value estimate of about S$2.53 and the analyst price target of about S$2.09, while offering a trailing dividend yield of 6.89% and trading on a 26.6x P/E that is above the 17.4x Asian industrial REIT average.
  • Analysts expecting around 15.7% yearly earnings growth and only 1.7% yearly revenue growth are effectively saying future margins and capital allocation need to do more of the work, which partly explains why valuation signals do not all point the same way.
    • The yield at 6.89% and the roughly 22% gap between the current price and DCF fair value support the idea of income plus some potential valuation upside, even after a year in which earnings included a S$128.5 million one off loss.
    • At the same time, the above industry P/E of 26.6x and weaker net profit margin of 31.4% compared to 47.2% a year earlier mean the market is already paying more per dollar of trailing earnings than peers, while the business is working through margin compression.

data.title: AIG CEO Succession Sets Tone For Underwriting Discipline And Capital Priorities
data.description:
• American International Group (NYSE:AIG) has completed its CEO succession plan.
  • Eric Andersen will become President and CEO, effective June 1.
  • Current CEO Peter Zaffino will transition to the role of Executive Chair.
  • Andersen will also join AIG's board of directors as part of the transition.

AIG is a large global insurer with operations across commercial and consumer insurance, as well as related financial services. For a company of this scale, a finalized leadership handover is a key development that can shape how it approaches underwriting, risk management, and capital allocation over time.

For you as an investor, this kind of leadership change is often a prompt to review how the company’s stated priorities line up with your own expectations. As Eric Andersen steps into the CEO role and Peter Zaffino moves to Executive Chair, upcoming management commentary, capital plans, and product focus areas may provide clearer information about how AIG intends to position itself within the broader insurance sector.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for American International Group by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on American International Group.

Does the team leading American International Group have what it takes? See our full breakdown of the management team's track record and compensation.

This leadership change locks in continuity at the top of AIG while subtly shifting roles. Eric Andersen brings senior insurance broking and risk advisory experience from Aon, which could influence how AIG thinks about client coverage, product design, and partnerships. With Peter Zaffino moving to Executive Chair, investors get a structure where the former CEO is still closely involved in board level oversight while day to day execution moves to Andersen. For you, the key question is how closely Andersen’s priorities align with the existing focus on underwriting discipline, expense control, and capital returns that many investors already watch at AIG. Changes in tone on topics like reinsurance usage, exposure to large commercial risks, and the balance between share buybacks and investment in technology will be useful signals of how this CEO transition feeds into the long term plan.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The American International Group Narrative

  • The narrative around AIG highlights a push toward digitalization, AI use in underwriting and claims, and tighter cost control. Andersen’s background in large scale insurance operations could support continued focus on these themes.
  • A leadership change at the top can challenge execution on portfolio optimization and underwriting rigor if priorities or risk appetite differ from Zaffino’s approach, especially compared with peers such as Chubb, Travelers, or Allianz.
  • The CEO succession itself is not explicitly reflected in the narrative. Board level oversight by Zaffino as Executive Chair, and how that shapes future technology and capital allocation decisions, may not yet be fully captured.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for American International Group to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ CEO transitions can introduce execution risk if there are shifts in underwriting discipline, appetite for large exposures, or cost control that differ from what shareholders have grown used to.
  • ⚠️ A change in leadership while AIG continues to invest in digital and AI tools could lead to delays or reprioritization of key projects if management focus is pulled in different directions.
  • 🎁 Andersen’s move into the CEO role alongside Zaffino as Executive Chair offers leadership continuity, which can help maintain consistency in risk management and capital allocation policies.
  • 🎁 A fresh CEO with board representation may bring new industry relationships and perspectives, potentially helping AIG compete more effectively against insurers such as Aon’s clients, Chubb, and AXA.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how Andersen frames AIG’s priorities on upcoming earnings calls or investor days, especially comments about underwriting standards, use of technology in pricing and claims, and any changes to capital return plans. Any updates to long term financial targets, expense reduction goals, or reinsurance strategy will help you judge whether this leadership shift is mainly about continuity or signals a different direction. It is also worth tracking how rating agencies and large institutional investors respond to the new structure, because their confidence often shapes how much flexibility a global insurer has in pursuing acquisitions or balance sheet moves.

Preferred P/E of 15.6x: Is it justified?

Juroku Financial Group is trading on a P/E of 15.6x, which sits above both its closest peers at 14.7x and the wider JP Banks industry at 14.5x, so the market is already paying a premium for its earnings.

The P/E multiple compares the current share price to earnings per share and is a quick way of seeing how much investors are paying for each unit of profit. For a bank like Juroku Financial Group, this often reflects expectations around future profitability, balance sheet quality and how durable earnings might be across a full cycle.

Here, the higher P/E suggests investors are accepting a richer price compared with similar banks, even though the company has grown earnings at 6.8% per year over the past 5 years and its most recent yearly earnings growth of 15.3% did not exceed the Banks industry, which recorded 34.5%. With a Return on Equity of 5% flagged as low and the SWS DCF model indicating the share price of ¥2,064 is above an estimated future cash flow value of ¥1,842.41, the current premium appears to reflect optimism that is already priced in.

Against the broader JP Banks industry, the P/E of 15.6x stands above the 14.5x average, which reinforces the idea that the stock is priced more expensively than many domestic peers rather than sitting at a discount on valuation grounds alone.
